abstract: The synthesis of high $sp^3$-rich molecular frameworks is a critical objective in medicinal chemistry for improving the pharmacokinetic properties of drug candidates. Among these, azabicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes (aza-BCHs) and bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs) are prominent bioisosteres for pyrrolidines and phenyl rings. This work describes an efficient "scaffold hopping" strategy to interconvert these two strained bicyclic systems via single-atom skeletal editing. The modular synthetic sequence utilizes an intramolecular visible-light-mediated [2+2] photocycloaddition of secondary enamides to construct multifunctionalized aza-BCHs. Subsequently, a nitrogen-deletion protocol employing anomeric amides facilitates deamination to yield bridge-functionalized BCPs. This approach utilizes the nitrogen atom as a temporary linchpin to template the formation of the strained bicyclic architecture before its traceless removal. The protocol is compatible with various aryl and heteroaryl substituents, providing access to 1,2- and 1,2,4-substituted BCPs, including those that are difficult to synthesize by traditional propellane strain-release methods. The utility of the method is further demonstrated through a gram-scale synthesis utilizing flow photochemistry.
